coredata.dc:title: Facile one pot pyrolysis synthesis of carbon quantum dots and graphene oxide nanomaterials: All carbon hybrids as eco-environmental lubricants for low friction and remarkable wear-resistance
coredata.dc:creator: Shang, Wangji
Cai, Tao
Zhang, Yunxiao
Liu, Dan
Liu, Shenggao
coredata.dc:description: All carbon hybrids containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene oxide (GO) have been prepared via one pot pyrolysis of citric acid (TDCA). Their relationships between microstructures, compositions and tribological peformance have been investigated. The TDCA as lubricants in PEG synthetic lube base oil exhibited remarkable friction-reducing in boundary lubrication. Importantly, the anti-wear enhanced significantly with increased loads, while the friction-reduction performance increased slightly. The excellent tribological performance is due to the synergistic effect of sphere-like CQDs and GOs absorbed on the sliding surface, Moreover, the high load induced more TDCA deposited on interface and the carbons inclined to degrade and generate more ordered structure, which protected interface better against undesirable friction and wear.
coredata.dcterms:subject: All carbon hybrid
Carbon quantum dots
Graphene oxide
Tribological performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of thermally decomposed citric acid (TDCA)
Tribological properties of TDCA with different pyrolysis duration
Tribological properties of TDCA with different concentration
Tribological properties of TDCA at different load
Surface analyses of the worn surface
Friction-reducing and anti-wear mechanism of TDCA nanocomposites additives

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) TDCA-0.5 h, (b) TDCA-1h and (c) TDCA-4h.
FTIR spectra of the CA with different pyrolysis duration.
XPS peaks fitting for (a) TDCA-0.5 h, (b) TDCA-1h, (c) TDCA-4h.

(a) Proposed structural evolutions of TDCA with different pyrolysis duration, (b) Photoluminescence intensity of TDCA with different pyrolysis duration and (c) Digital images of dispersion of TDCA-1h nanocomposites in PEG base oil. The first sample bottle contains pure PEG and the remaining sample bottles contain 1.0 wt% TDCA-1h nanocomposites. The storage duration for each image is noted on the sample bottle respectively (1H: 1 h; 1 M: 1 month; 3 M: 3 month; 6 M: 6 month.).

(a) Friction coefficient curves and (b) mean friction coefficient and mean wear volume lubricated by 1.0 wt% TDCA in PEG with various pyrolysis durations. (load: 392 N; temperature 75 °C; rotate speed: 1200 rpm; duration: 60 min).

(a) Friction coefficient curves and (b) mean friction coefficient and mean wear volume lubricated by TDCA in PEG with various concentrations (load: 392 N; temperature 75 °C; rotate speed: 1200 rpm; duration: 60 min).

(a) mean coefficient friction, (b) mean wear volume lubricated by 1.0 wt% TDCA-1h in PEG solution at different load and (c) 3D images of PEG (c1-c3) and TDCA-1h (d1-d3) in PEG at selected applied load of 196 N, 392 N and 588 N. (rotate speed: 1200 rpm; temperature 75 °C; duration: 60 min).

SEM images, Carbon element and Raman spectrum for the worn surfaces of the upper ball lubricated by pure PEG at 392 N (p1, p2, p3, p4). SEM images for the worn surfaces of the upper ball under lubrication at 196 N (a1, a2), 392 N (b1, b2), 588 N (c1, c2). Carbon element mapping of the worn surfaces (a3,b3,c3). Raman spectrum of the worn surfaces of TDCA nanocomposites at load of 196 N, 392 N and 588 N (a4, b4, c4).
Schematic illustration of the friction-reducing and anti-wear mechanism of TDCA as additive under boundary lubrication.
C1s bonding states assignment from XPS peaks fitting and carbon-oxygen ratio at different pyrolysis duration.
The surface elemental content of worn area lubricated by1.0 wt% TDCA-1h in PEG200 at 196 N, 392 N, 588 N.

head.ce:title: Facile one pot pyrolysis synthesis of carbon quantum dots and graphene oxide nanomaterials: All carbon hybrids as eco-environmental lubricants for low friction and remarkable wear-resistance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Industrial Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, No. 1219, Zhongguan West Road, Ningbo City, Zhejiang Province 315201, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, No. 99, Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: All carbon hybrids containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene oxide (GO) have been prepared via one pot pyrolysis of citric acid (TDCA). Their relationships between microstructures, compositions and tribological peformance have been investigated. The TDCA as lubricants in PEG synthetic lube base oil exhibited remarkable friction-reducing in boundary lubrication. Importantly, the anti-wear enhanced significantly with increased loads, while the friction-reduction performance increased slightly. The excellent tribological performance is due to the synergistic effect of sphere-like CQDs and GOs absorbed on the sliding surface, Moreover, the high load induced more TDCA deposited on interface and the carbons inclined to degrade and generate more ordered structure, which protected interface better against undesirable friction and wear.

The relationship between PL properties, the microstructures and tribological performance of as-synthesized hybrid has been established.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: According to the estimation, there are 30–40% of the fuel energy consumed against friction and wear in modern industry [1]. This is the reason why researches in tribology have focused on the development of low friction materials and more effective lubricant additives. Conventional lubricant additives contained phosphorus, sulphur and chloride element, are being used as multifunctional additives such as anti-wear, extreme pressure and anti-oxidant, but they cannot meet the requirements of environmental protection regulations [2–4]. Over the last decade, inorganic nanoparticles, such as carbon materials [5–9], h-BN [10–13], MoS2 [14–16] have been applied in lubrication areas for improving tribological properties, saving energy and protecting the machinery and equipment. Carbon materials included fullerene, carbon nanotubes, and graphene as well as their hybrids or derivatives have been widely used as solid lubricants or oil additives owing to their unique self-lubricating effects [17–19]. Among them, graphene oxide has been most studied nanomaterials in various fields nowadays due to its unprecedented properties. In tribology field, graphene oxide has been studied as an emerging lubricant owing to its lamellar structure, which provides low inter-lamellar shear and sliding friction. However, the application is severely limited due to the complex synthetic methods and high cost, importantly, due to the strong van der Waals force and π-π stacking interactions of GO thus result in their re-stacking, leading to the concerns of dispersibility and long term storage stability of GO in polar and non-polar medium worse [20–23]. Therefore, fabrication of stable GO dispersion, for enhanced tribological performance and practical application is of great significance.

As a new kind of carbon materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have rapidly attracted great research owing to their unique properties such as outstanding optical properties, robust chemical inertness and good biocompatibility, etc. [24–31]. According to the lubrication theory and microstructure, the CQDs could act as a promising material in tribological communities due to itself size effect, high specific surface area and controllable functionalized surface, in addition, the dispersibility and storage stability could be improved significantly comparing with that of conventional carbon materials in polar medium. Very recently, there are limited several CQDs materials have been developed for tribological applications based on surface functionalization [32,33]. However, there are some concerns such as complex synthetic approaches, cost, tribological performance and their biocompatibility with environment should be improved for further application perspective. On the other hand, considering the conjugated π structure, CQDs may exhibit strong non-covalent π-π interactivities with GOs, which provide a more stable dispersion system [34–37]. Therefore, it is promising that the CQDs/GO hybrid as lubricants may effectively enhance tribological properties in terms of low friction and excellent wear-resistance. However, to the best of our knowledge, both syntheses of CQDs/GO hybrid nanomaterials through one pot pyrolysis and their “structure-property” relationship as lubricants have scarely been reported.

Herein, we reported a facile and scalable method for synthesis of an all carbon hybrids composed of CQDs and GO with a low cost method namely “bottom-up” using environmentally friendly citric acid (CA) as precursor, which occurs naturally in citrus fruits. The citric acid (CA) was used to selectively produce thermally decomposed citric acid (TDCA) containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and/or graphene oxide (GO) by tuning the carbonization degree. We suppose that the moiety of GO in small quantity improves the lubrication characters by reducing the friction and wear between the steel tribo-pairs, while the moiety of CQDs in large quantity facilitate both the dispersion and better tribological performance of CQDs/GO hybrid. The relationships between the microstructure, chemical composition and tribological performance of TDCA have been investigated in a broad load under boundary lubrication. It is anticipated that a stable dispersed carbon-based nanoparticles may serve as a novel and high performance lubricant additive for future lubrication application.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5%) and PEG with an average molecular weight of 190–210 g mol−1 (AR grade) was obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All the chemicals were used as received without further purification. The properties of PEG synthetic lube base oil were shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of thermally decomposed citric acid (TDCA)
ce:section.ce:para: A 250 ml round flask was placed into a silicone oil bath. After the oil bath was heated to 210 °C, 10 g of citric acid was added into the flask. About 5min later, the CA was liquated. Subsequently, the colour of the liquid was changed from colourless to pale yellow, and then orange in 0.5 h, indicating the initial formation of CQDs. When the heating was kept on, the orange liquid would turn to puce liquid in about 1 h. Finally, it would turn black solid in about 4 h, suggesting transformed into GO completely. The obtained samples abbreviated as TDCA-th when the pyrolysis time (t) was 0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the TDCA-th was dissolved in deionized water and then centrifugated for removing few sediment (10000 rpm, 30min). Dialyzing the supernatant with molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000 against deionized water for 72 h. The solvent was removed by rotary evaporator, and residual dried under vacuum (20 °C) for 72 h.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the TDCA were recorded on a HRTEM JEOL2100 system operating at 200 kV. XPS results were acquired on a AXIS ULTRA Spectrometer. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by a BRUKER AXS D8 DISCOVER using Cu Ka radiation. The morphologies and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectra of worn surfaces were obtained on a scanning electron microscope (SEM, EVO MA15) operating at 20 kV. Fluorescence spectra were collected through a Fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Lumina). 3D image and surface roughness were measured by confocal laser scanning microscopy (Zeiss LSM700). Raman spectra were acquired on a Renishaw in Via Reflex using a 532 nm laser.

The friction and wear tests were conducted on a four-ball tester. The four-ball test was performed in a ball-on-ball configuration by oscillating an AISI52100 steel ball (diameter, 12.7 mm; hardness, 59–61HRC; roughness, <0.032 μm) over three identical balls under the following conditions: rotating rate, 1200 rpm; duration, 60 min; temperature 75 °C; load, 196–588 N, relative humidity, 50–70%). The oil cup was washed by petroleum ether and ethanol, and then the specimens were added to the oil cup. The mean wear scar diameter (WSD) of the three lower balls was measured using an optical microscope, and mean friction coefficients were recorded automatically by a computer using a data-acquiring system linked to the friction and wear tester.

To estimate the wear volume (WV) for the three balls after the tribo-tests, we used the following equation:
V =
(
π h
6
)
(
3
d 2
4
+
h 2
)
Where
h = r −
r 2
−
d 2
4
d is wear scar diameter and r is the radius of the ball.

To investigate the morphologies, PL properties and chemical components of as-synthesized nanomaterials, the evolution process of TDCA was identified with TEM, FTIR, XRD, fluorescence spectra and XPS, while TDCA with pyrolysis duration of 0.5 h, 1 h and 4 h were selected as typical samples. As shown in Fig. 1 a–c, the TDCA-0.5 h are spherical CQDs exhibiting uniform and well dispersed with an average diameter of approximately 2.5 nm (Fig. 1a). Prolonging pyrolysis duration to 1 h, hybrid structure of TDCA-1h containing CQDs in large quantity and GO in small quantity was obtained, which is similar with previous studies (Fig. 1b) [38]. It is found that TDCA-4h are sheets with hundreds of nanometers in size, suggesting the evolution of GO (Fig. 1c). Therefore, the TDCA-th (where t = 0.5, 1, 4) could be denoted as CQDs, hybrid and GO respectively in this work. In addition, the TDCA exhibit a poor degree of crystallinity according to the XRD result (Fig. S1).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) was used to characterize the citric acid (CA) and thermally decomposed citric acid (TDCA) with pyrolysis duration of 0.5 h, 1 h and 4 h. As shown in Fig. 2 . CA monomer exhibited typical strong absorption peak of hydroxyl group at 3440 cm−1 and stretching vibration absorption peak of C-H at 2980 cm−1 as well as stretching vibration peak of C-H in the range of 1400 cm−1. When pyrolysis duration was prolonged, the typical peaks of CA were gradually weakened, while the stretching vibration peak of C=C at 1650 cm−1, bending vibration of C=CH2 at 890 cm−1 and absorption peak of hydroxyl group at 3440 cm−1 as well as stretching vibration absorption peak of C-H at 2980 cm−1 were gradually weakened, suggesting that the degree of carbonation gradually enhanced with the increase of pyrolysis duration. The new peak centred at 1845 cm−1 contributed to anhydrides was gradually enhanced, suggesting the -OH transformed into anhydrides during pyrolysis. There were extremely weak peaks of C-H observed at TDCA with pyrolysis duration of 4 h, suggesting high carbonization of CA thus produced GO.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the XPS fine structure spectra of C1s exhibited four main peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.8 eV, 288.7 eV and 289.2 eV, which were attributed to C-C/C=C, C-OH, C=O and -COOH respectively. The XPS spectra confirmed the carbon-oxygen ratio of TDCA increased with the increase of pyrolysis duration. With the extension of pyrolysis duration, the amount of C-OH, C=O and -COOH decreased, whereas the amount of C-C/C=C bonding increased. Detailed percentage of all observed chemical bonds in analysed samples were provided in Table 1 , the degree of carbonation gradually enhanced with the increase of pyrolysis duration, which was consistent with the results from FT-IR.

The structural evolution in the proportion of the CQDs and GO particles in TDCA can be understood in Fig. 4 and Fig. S2. A clear understanding on the evolution trends in the thermal decomposition process can be obtained from above results and changes of the PL properties. Schematic represented the thermal decomposition process of monomer citric acid (Fig. 4a): Firstly, CQDs derived from polycondensed monomer citric acid and left a few of citric acid, which possessed excitation-independent and strong PL (Fig. S2a). Secondly, more monomer citric acid would be converted to form CQDs constantly, meanwhile a part of the small CQDs could transform into GO with a large scale. There is a certain proportion of CQDs and GO in the system, which show a certain excitation-independent PL (Fig. S2b); Prolonging the pyrolysis duration, most of the CQDs converted into GO, which showed excitation-dependent PL properties (Fig. S2c). Hence, it is dominated by the inhomogeneous larger particles of the excitation dependent emission. Eventually, no small size CQDs could be detected and fluorescence weakened and even disappeared (Fig. S2d). From evolution of thermal decomposition process, CQDs are also reduced and GO increased. In our study, the synthesized of TDCA reveals significant information on the structural evolution with tuning carbonization degree.

The TDCA-1h could be directly dissolved in PEG without any dispersants, ultrasound shocks, and as-prepared clear solution exhibited a long term homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation, which could be attributed to similar polarity between the TDCA-1h and PEG. It was noted that TDCA-1h still possessed photoluminescence properties after 6 months, indicating ultrahigh dispersion stability of as-synthesized hybrid materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological properties of TDCA with different pyrolysis duration
ce:section.ce:para: The tribological properties of 1.0 wt% TDCA-th (where t present pyrolysis duration, t = 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4) as lubricants in PEG synthetic lube base oil were tested using a four-ball model for steel/steel contact in boundary lubrication. The friction coefficient and wear volume of lower balls was shown in Fig. 5 . It was found that the raw citric acid before pyrolysis behaved terrible friction and wear properties, whereas TDCA-th worked better than that of CA. The variation tendency for the friction coefficient and wear volume decreased sharply when t ≤ 1, and then increased slightly when t ≥ 1. It was found that the TDCA-1h showed the lowest friction coefficient while the curve became smooth and exhibited a downtrend. The friction coefficient of TDCA-th (t = 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4) in PEG were 0.129, 0.057, 0.030, 0.037, 0.056, meaning 55.8%, 76.7%, 71.3%, 56.6% improvement than that of raw CA in PEG. A similar variation tendency for the wear volume were observed and the wear volume of TDCA-xh (x = 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4) in PEG were 6.80 × 10−3mm3, 2.81 × 10−3mm3, 1.92 × 10−3mm3, 2.35 × 10−3mm3, 2.90 × 10−3mm3, meaning 58.7%, 71.8%, 65.4%, 57.4%, improvement than that of raw CA in PEG. The TDCA-1h as lubricant exhibited the lowest mean friction coefficient and wear volume, meaning the best tribological performance obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological properties of TDCA with different concentration
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the relationship between the tribological properties and concentration, the curves of friction coefficient and wear volume of TDCA-1h-c (where c present concentration, c = 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4) with different concentrations in PEG were shown in Fig. 6 . It was shown that the friction coefficient firstly decreased sharply, when c ≥ 1, and then increase slightly with the increasing concentrations. The friction coefficient of PEG with TDCA-1h-c (c = 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4) were 0.095, 0.057, 0.048, 0.030, 0.033, 0.039, meaning 9.52%, 45.7%, 54.3%, 71.4%, 68.6%, 62.9% improvement than PEG. In addition, the variation tendency for the wear volume was different, which firstly decreased sharply (c ≤ 0.5) and then decreased slightly (c ≥ 1) with the increase of concentrations. The wear volume of PEG with TDCA-1h-c (c = 0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4) were 3.26 × 10−3mm3, 2.32 × 10−3mm3, 2.11 × 10−3mm3, 1.92 × 10−3mm3, 1.68 × 10−3mm3, 1.49 × 10−3mm3, meaning 43.8%, 60.0%, 63.6%, 66.9%, 71.0%, 74.3% improvement than PEG. The lowest coefficient of friction was obtained as the 1.0 wt% TDCA-1h introduced. These results indicated the low concentrations of TDCA could enhance tribological performance of PEG synthetic lube base oil, leading to a significant reduce in the mean friction coefficient and wear volume.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological properties of TDCA at different load
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further explore the tribological properties of 1.0 wt% TDCA-1h, a different applied loads were employed with TDCA-1h-p (where p present load, p = 196 N, 294 N, 392 N, 490 N, 588 N) in PEG investigated (Fig. 7 ). It was found that the TDCA-1h as lubricant additive could effectively enhance the anti-wear ability and load bearing capacity of PEG. The friction coefficient of PEG were 0.102, 0.103, 0.105, 0.113, 0.120 at different loads, while the friction coefficient of PEG with TDCA-1h-p (p = 196 N, 294 N, 392 N, 490 N, 588 N) were 0.040, 0.031, 0.030, 0.037, 0.039, meaning 60.8%, 69.9%, 71.4%, 67.2%, 67.5% improvement than that of PEG. On the other hand, the wear volume of PEG were 0.867 × 10−3mm3, 1.47 × 10−3mm3, 5.80 × 10−3mm3, 9.57 × 10−3mm3, 18.8 × 10−3 mm3 at different loads, while the wear volume of PEG with TDCA-1h-p (p = 196 N, 294 N, 392 N, 490 N, 588 N) were 0.851 × 10−3mm3, 1.26 × 10−3mm3, 1.92 × 10−3mm3, 2.29 × 10−3mm3, 3.11 × 10−3mm3, meaning 1.85%, 14.3%, 66.9%, 76.1%, 83.5% improvement than that of PEG. It was evident that a remarkable improvement in wear-resistance for TDCA achieved with the rise of applied loads.

These 3D images clearly indicated the anti-wear properties of the TDCA-1h as lubricant additives in PEG. The balls lubricated by the PEG alone had acquired significantly larger and deeper wear scar, obviously, wear scar decreased distinctly when the TDCA-1h was introduced at selected applied load of 196 N, 392 N and 588 N. 3D optical microscope was also used to investigate the surface morphologies of neat PEG with and without TDCA-1h. When the load was 196 N, 392 N and 588 N, the surface roughness (Ra) of PEG was 5.834 μm, 6.868 μm and 7.353 μm, while the value for TDCA-1h was 5.008 μm, 6.501 μm and 6.544 μm. It was evident the surface roughness decreased significantly with the introducing of TDCA-1h, which indicated the boundary films formed by CQDs and GO could inhibit undesired friction and wear effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface analyses of the worn surface
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand worn surface morphology and element distribution, scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy dispersive X-spectrometer (EDS) as well as Raman spectra were employed to characterize the corresponding wear scars after ultrasonic washing the surface and identify the lubricaiting mechanism, as shown in Fig. 8 and Table 2 , the distribution and content of carbon from SEM-EDS and mappings showed the surface lubricated by TDCA possessing some dark colour active areas and higher carbon concentration, which may indicated the CQDs and GO in TDCA had strong polarity and adsorbed on the worn ball surface thus fulfilling the better tribological properties against wear and friction. The carbon forms derived from TDCA on the worn surface is further identified with micro-Raman spectroscopy of the upper ball at 196 N, 392 N and 588 N. It was found that multiple peaks below 700 cm−1 observed in all three spectra, which were attributed to iron oxides of FeO, Fe2O3 and Fe3O4, indicating the oxidation process happened on the interface during test [39]. Fig. 8(a4) showed the Raman spectroscopy of the upper ball lubricated by TDCA at 196 N, it was clearly that D peak centred at 1390 cm−1 and broadened 2D peak centred at 2455 cm−1 observed while no G peak was found, indicating the carbons deposited on the worn surface with disorder and defect structure due to edge effects. The Raman signal for the deposited carbon layer is entirely transformed with the rise of load. Fig. 8(b4) showed the Raman spectroscopy of the upper ball lubricated by TDCA at 392 N, the intensity of D peak centred at 1390 cm−1 increased obviously, besides, two new Raman peaks, G peak centred at 1590 cm−1 and D + D′ peak centred at 2880 cm−1 appeared, indicating the deposited carbons transformed into ordered structure with substantial defects. After the tribo-tests at 588 N (Fig. 8(c4)), both D and G peaks increased further while G peaks shifted to 1610 cm−1. All these changes indicated the carbons derived from TDCA had high reactivity with steel interface which could deposit on interface quickly for the formation of carbon layer tribo-film thus contributing to excellent friction-reducing performance. Moreover, the deposited carbons could further decompose and produce more defects and orderly carbon forms thus contributing to significantly reducing in friction and wear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Friction-reducing and anti-wear mechanism of TDCA nanocomposites additives
ce:section.ce:para: We report the preparation of TDCA nanomaterials herein, which exhibit high solubility and chemical stability in PEG. Moreover, the tribological performance results indicate that TDCA nanomaterials can significantly reduce friction coefficient and wear scar diameter in boundary lubrication. Based on the above results, we put forward a reasonable lubrication mechanism of TDCA based nanomaterials. In the appropriate proportion of CQDs and GO, the hybrid can play the significant role of friction-reducing and anti-wear lubrication than that of CQDs and GO alone. In a word, it shows an ultralow friction coefficient due to the synergistic lubricating effect of CQDs and GO. As shown in Fig. 9 , under boundary lubrication, it shall occur intensive tribo-chemical reaction by the active group of TDCA between the shear surfaces and form tribo-chemical reaction film [40]. In this case, TDCA nanomaterials exhibit “self-mending effect” by filling the deep scratches, which directly accounts for excellent anti-wear performances. When load is high enough, the carbons in tribo-chemical film become ordered forms during the tribo-tests which are contributed to better tribological performance against friction and wear.

We have reported a facile and scalable “bottom-up” method for synthesis of an all carbon hybrid lubricant materials, which is composed of carbon quantum dots and graphene oxide hybrid using citric acid (CA) as only ecofriendly carbon source. The TDCA containing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and/or graphene oxide (GO) was obtained by tuning the carbonization degree of CA, which exhibited good solubility in PEG synthetic lube base oil. The as-obtained clear solution exhibited a long term homogeneous phase of over 6 months without any noticeable precipitation. When the TDCA as lubricant additive, it showed an improved apparently lubrication performance in broad loads, importantly, TDCA containing CQDs/GO hybrid exhibited optimal tribology performance which reduced the friction coefficient of 60.8–71.4%, Moreover, the anti-wear effect increased up from 1.85% to 83.5% with the load up to 588 N. All the results imply that the synergistic effect of CQDs and GO on the interface, the deposited carbons derived from TDCA further decompose and produce more defects and orderly carbon structure in tribo-chemical film thus contributing to the enhanced reducing in wear and friction. The present work demonstrates the great potential application of the pristine carbon-based nanomaterials as a kind of designable and high effectively nano-additive for diversified tribological applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by “Thousand Talents Program”, National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21404114), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grand No. 2014M561797), and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo (Grant No. 2015A610051).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Manuscript SI R1 Manuscript SI R1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.triboint.2017.09.029.
